summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Levels of Organization (Cells → Organism)
summary_sections.content_html:
Levels of Organization describe how the body is built from simple to complex:

  • Chemical level: atoms and molecules (e.g., water, proteins, DNA)
  • Cellular level: the basic unit of life; cells carry out specialized functions
  • Tissue level: groups of similar cells working together (epithelial, connective, muscle, nervous)
  • Organ level: two or more tissue types forming a structure with a specific function (e.g., heart, stomach)
  • Organ system level: organs cooperating for a common purpose (e.g., digestive system)
  • Organism level: all systems working together to sustain life

flashcards.question: What is the smallest level of body organization?
flashcards.answer: The chemical level — atoms combine to form molecules

flashcards.question: What is a tissue?
flashcards.answer: A group of similar cells that work together to perform a specific function

flashcards.question: What is an organ?
flashcards.answer: A structure composed of two or more tissue types that performs a specific function

flashcards.question: What is an organ system?
flashcards.answer: A group of organs that cooperate to accomplish a common purpose

flashcards.question: What is the difference between an organ and an organ system?
flashcards.answer: An organ is a single structure (e.g., heart); an organ system is multiple organs working together (e.g., cardiovascular system)

quiz_questions.question_text: The correct order from simplest to most complex is:

options.text: Chemical → Cell → Tissue → Organ → System → Organism

quiz_questions.explanation: The hierarchy goes: chemical → cellular → tissue → organ → organ system → organism.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which level of organization does the stomach represent?
quiz_questions.explanation: The stomach is made of multiple tissue types (epithelial, muscle, connective, nervous) working together, making it an organ.

quiz_questions.question_text: Epithelial, connective, muscle, and nervous are the four types of:
quiz_questions.explanation: These are the four fundamental tissue types in the human body.

quiz_questions.question_text: A group of organs working together for a common purpose is called:
quiz_questions.explanation: An organ system (e.g., the digestive system) consists of multiple organs cooperating.

quiz_questions.question_text: DNA is an example of which level of organization?
quiz_questions.explanation: DNA is a molecule, which belongs to the chemical level of organization.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which organ system includes the heart, blood vessels, and blood?
quiz_questions.explanation: The cardiovascular (circulatory) system consists of the heart, blood vessels, and blood.

quiz_questions.question_text: The basic unit of life is the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The cell is the smallest functional unit of life, capable of carrying out life processes.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Anatomical Terminology & Body Planes
summary_sections.content_html:
Anatomical terminology provides a universal language for describing body locations and directions:

  • Anatomical position: body erect, facing forward, palms forward, feet together
  • Superior/Inferior: toward the head / toward the feet
  • Anterior (ventral)/Posterior (dorsal): front / back of the body
  • Medial/Lateral: toward the midline / away from the midline
  • Proximal/Distal: closer to / farther from the point of attachment
  • Body planes: Sagittal (left/right), Frontal/Coronal (front/back), Transverse (top/bottom)

flashcards.question: What is anatomical position?
flashcards.answer: Standing erect, facing forward, arms at sides, palms facing forward

flashcards.question: What does 'superior' mean?
flashcards.answer: Toward the head or upper part of the body

flashcards.question: What is the sagittal plane?
flashcards.answer: A vertical plane that divides the body into left and right portions

flashcards.question: What does 'proximal' mean?
flashcards.answer: Closer to the point of attachment or the trunk of the body

flashcards.question: What is the transverse plane?
flashcards.answer: A horizontal plane that divides the body into upper (superior) and lower (inferior) portions

quiz_questions.question_text: In anatomical position, the palms face:
quiz_questions.explanation: In the standard anatomical position, the palms face anteriorly (forward).

quiz_questions.question_text: The term 'medial' means:

options.text: Toward the midline of the body

quiz_questions.explanation: Medial means closer to the body's midline.

quiz_questions.question_text: A sagittal plane divides the body into:
quiz_questions.explanation: The sagittal plane runs vertically and separates left from right.

quiz_questions.question_text: The elbow is _____ to the wrist.
quiz_questions.explanation: The elbow is closer to the trunk (proximal) compared to the wrist.

quiz_questions.question_text: 'Anterior' is also known as:
quiz_questions.explanation: Anterior (front of the body) is synonymous with ventral in humans.

quiz_questions.question_text: A transverse (horizontal) plane divides the body into:
quiz_questions.explanation: The transverse plane cuts horizontally, creating upper and lower sections.

quiz_questions.question_text: The knee is _____ to the hip.
quiz_questions.explanation: The knee is below (inferior to) the hip.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Homeostasis & Feedback Mechanisms
summary_sections.content_html:
Homeostasis is the body's ability to maintain stable internal conditions despite external changes.

  • Negative feedback: most common mechanism; the response opposes the stimulus to restore balance (e.g., body temperature regulation, blood glucose regulation)
  • Positive feedback: the response amplifies the stimulus; less common (e.g., blood clotting, childbirth contractions)
  • Components: receptor (detects change) → control center (processes information) → effector (carries out response)
  • Failure of homeostasis can lead to disease or death

flashcards.question: What is homeostasis?
flashcards.answer: The body's ability to maintain a stable internal environment despite external changes

flashcards.question: What is negative feedback?
flashcards.answer: A mechanism where the response opposes the stimulus to return to a set point

flashcards.question: Give an example of positive feedback.
flashcards.answer: Blood clotting — the clotting process amplifies itself until the wound is sealed

flashcards.question: What are the three components of a feedback loop?
flashcards.answer: Receptor (detects change), control center (processes info), effector (carries out response)

flashcards.question: What happens when homeostasis fails?
flashcards.answer: Disease, disorder, or potentially death can result

quiz_questions.question_text: Homeostasis is best defined as:

options.text: The movement of blood through the body

quiz_questions.explanation: Homeostasis refers to the body's maintenance of stable internal conditions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Most feedback mechanisms in the body are:
quiz_questions.explanation: Negative feedback is far more common; it counteracts changes to maintain stability.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is an example of positive feedback?
quiz_questions.explanation: Childbirth contractions amplify — each contraction triggers stronger ones — classic positive feedback.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a feedback loop, the receptor:

options.text: Detects the change in the environment

quiz_questions.explanation: The receptor senses the stimulus or change from the set point.

quiz_questions.question_text: When body temperature rises, sweat glands activate to cool the body. This is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Sweating opposes the temperature rise, bringing the body back toward its set point — negative feedback.

quiz_questions.question_text: The control center in thermoregulation is the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The hypothalamus acts as the body's thermostat, processing temperature information and triggering responses.

quiz_questions.question_text: If homeostasis is disrupted and not restored, the result can be:
quiz_questions.explanation: Persistent failure of homeostasis leads to pathological conditions and can be fatal.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Cell Structure & Function Review
summary_sections.content_html:
Cell Structure & Function Review:

  • Cell membrane: phospholipid bilayer; selectively permeable barrier controlling what enters and exits
  • Nucleus: contains DNA; directs cell activities and stores genetic information
  • Mitochondria: 'powerhouses' that produce ATP through cellular respiration
  • Ribosomes: synthesize proteins from mRNA instructions
  • Endoplasmic reticulum: rough ER (with ribosomes) processes proteins; smooth ER synthesizes lipids
  • Golgi apparatus: packages, modifies, and ships proteins and lipids
  • Lysosomes: contain enzymes for intracellular digestion
  • Cytoplasm: gel-like fluid filling the cell; site of many metabolic reactions

flashcards.question: What is the function of mitochondria?
flashcards.answer: To produce ATP (energy) through cellular respiration — the 'powerhouse' of the cell

flashcards.question: What does the cell membrane do?
flashcards.answer: Acts as a selectively permeable barrier controlling substances entering and leaving the cell

flashcards.question: What is the role of ribosomes?
flashcards.answer: Synthesize (build) proteins by reading mRNA instructions

flashcards.question: What does the Golgi apparatus do?
flashcards.answer: Packages, modifies, and distributes proteins and lipids for transport

flashcards.question: What is the function of lysosomes?
flashcards.answer: Contain digestive enzymes that break down waste, cellular debris, and foreign invaders

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'powerhouse of the cell' is the:
quiz_questions.explanation: Mitochondria generate most of the cell's ATP through cellular respiration.

quiz_questions.question_text: DNA is primarily found in the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The nucleus houses the cell's DNA and directs cellular activities.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which organelle synthesizes proteins?
quiz_questions.explanation: Ribosomes translate mRNA into amino acid chains (proteins).

quiz_questions.question_text: The cell membrane is described as 'selectively permeable' because it:

options.text: Allows some substances through while blocking others

quiz_questions.explanation: Selective permeability means the membrane controls which molecules can cross.

quiz_questions.question_text: Rough ER differs from smooth ER because rough ER has:
quiz_questions.explanation: Ribosomes on the rough ER surface give it a 'rough' appearance and enable protein processing.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which organelle is responsible for packaging and shipping proteins?
quiz_questions.explanation: The Golgi apparatus modifies, sorts, and packages proteins for transport.

quiz_questions.question_text: Lysosomes function as the cell's:
quiz_questions.explanation: Lysosomes contain hydrolytic enzymes that digest worn-out organelles, debris, and bacteria.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Tissue Types (Epithelial, Connective, Muscle, Nervous)
summary_sections.content_html:
The body has four fundamental tissue types:

  • Epithelial tissue: covers body surfaces and lines cavities; functions in protection, absorption, secretion, and filtration. Classified by cell shape (squamous, cuboidal, columnar) and layers (simple, stratified).
  • Connective tissue: most abundant type; supports, binds, and protects. Includes bone, blood, cartilage, adipose (fat), and tendons/ligaments.
  • Muscle tissue: generates movement. Three types: skeletal (voluntary, striated), cardiac (involuntary, striated, intercalated discs), smooth (involuntary, non-striated, in organs).
  • Nervous tissue: transmits electrical signals. Composed of neurons (conduct impulses) and neuroglia (support cells).

flashcards.question: What are the four tissue types?
flashcards.answer: Epithelial, connective, muscle, and nervous tissue

flashcards.question: What is the most abundant tissue type?
flashcards.answer: Connective tissue — it includes bone, blood, cartilage, and fat

flashcards.question: What are the three types of muscle tissue?
flashcards.answer: Skeletal (voluntary), cardiac (heart), and smooth (organs) muscle

flashcards.question: What makes cardiac muscle unique?
flashcards.answer: It is striated like skeletal muscle but involuntary, and has intercalated discs for coordinated heartbeats

flashcards.question: What are the two cell types in nervous tissue?
flashcards.answer: Neurons (transmit impulses) and neuroglia (support cells)

quiz_questions.question_text: Which tissue type covers body surfaces and lines cavities?
quiz_questions.explanation: Epithelial tissue forms protective coverings and linings throughout the body.

quiz_questions.question_text: Blood is classified as which tissue type?
quiz_questions.explanation: Blood is a fluid connective tissue with cells suspended in plasma.

quiz_questions.question_text: Skeletal muscle is different from smooth muscle because skeletal muscle is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Skeletal muscle is under conscious (voluntary) control and has visible striations.

quiz_questions.question_text: Intercalated discs are a unique feature of:
quiz_questions.explanation: Intercalated discs connect cardiac muscle cells, allowing synchronized contraction of the heart.

quiz_questions.question_text: Neurons are cells that:
quiz_questions.explanation: Neurons are specialized to generate and conduct nerve impulses.

quiz_questions.question_text: Stratified squamous epithelium is found in the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The skin's epidermis is stratified squamous epithelium — multiple layers of flat cells for protection.

quiz_questions.question_text: Neuroglia (glial cells) function to:
quiz_questions.explanation: Neuroglia support, insulate, and nourish neurons in the nervous system.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Bone Structure & Classification
summary_sections.content_html:
Bone Structure & Classification:

  • Bones are living organs made of compact bone (dense outer layer) and spongy bone (porous inner layer with trabeculae)
  • Periosteum: tough membrane covering the bone surface; contains blood vessels and nerves
  • Bone cells: osteoblasts (build bone), osteoclasts (break down bone), osteocytes (mature bone cells maintaining the matrix)
  • Classification by shape: long (femur), short (carpals), flat (skull), irregular (vertebrae), sesamoid (patella)
  • Bones store calcium and phosphorus, produce blood cells in red marrow, and store fat in yellow marrow

flashcards.question: What are osteoblasts?
flashcards.answer: Bone-building cells that deposit new bone matrix

flashcards.question: What are osteoclasts?
flashcards.answer: Cells that break down (resorb) bone tissue

flashcards.question: What is the periosteum?
flashcards.answer: A tough fibrous membrane covering the outer surface of bone, containing blood vessels and nerves

flashcards.question: What is compact bone?
flashcards.answer: Dense, hard outer layer of bone that provides strength and protection

flashcards.question: Name the five bone classifications by shape.
flashcards.answer: Long, short, flat, irregular, and sesamoid

quiz_questions.question_text: Osteoblasts function to:
quiz_questions.explanation: Osteoblasts are bone-forming cells that synthesize and deposit new bone matrix.

quiz_questions.question_text: The femur is classified as a _____ bone.
quiz_questions.explanation: The femur (thigh bone) is a classic example of a long bone.

quiz_questions.question_text: Red blood cells are produced in:
quiz_questions.explanation: Red bone marrow is the site of hematopoiesis (blood cell production).

quiz_questions.question_text: The tough membrane covering the outside of a bone is the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The periosteum is a fibrous sheath covering the external surface of bones.

quiz_questions.question_text: Spongy bone contains:
quiz_questions.explanation: Spongy bone has a lattice-like structure of bony plates called trabeculae.

quiz_questions.question_text: Bones serve all the following functions EXCEPT:
quiz_questions.explanation: Bones store minerals, produce blood cells, and protect organs; while they do produce some hormones, this is not their exclusive function.

quiz_questions.question_text: Osteocytes are:

options.text: Mature bone cells embedded in the matrix

quiz_questions.explanation: Osteocytes are mature osteoblasts trapped within the bone matrix that maintain bone tissue.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: The Axial Skeleton
summary_sections.content_html:
The axial skeleton (80 bones) forms the body's central axis:

  • Skull (22 bones): cranium (8 bones protecting the brain) and facial bones (14 bones)
  • Vertebral column (26 bones): 7 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, 1 sacrum (5 fused), 1 coccyx (4 fused). Cushioned by intervertebral discs.
  • Thoracic cage: 12 pairs of ribs + sternum. Protects heart and lungs. True ribs (1–7) attach directly to sternum; false ribs (8–12) do not; floating ribs (11–12) have no anterior attachment.
  • Hyoid bone: supports the tongue; only bone not articulating with another bone

flashcards.question: How many bones are in the axial skeleton?
flashcards.answer: 80 bones

flashcards.question: How many cervical vertebrae are there?
flashcards.answer: 7 cervical vertebrae (in the neck)

flashcards.question: What are true ribs?
flashcards.answer: Ribs 1–7 that attach directly to the sternum via costal cartilage

flashcards.question: What is the hyoid bone?
flashcards.answer: A U-shaped bone in the neck that supports the tongue; the only bone not articulating with another bone

flashcards.question: What is the function of intervertebral discs?
flashcards.answer: They cushion and absorb shock between vertebrae, allowing flexibility

quiz_questions.question_text: The axial skeleton consists of approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: The axial skeleton has 80 bones including the skull, vertebral column, and thoracic cage.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many cervical vertebrae are in the human spine?
quiz_questions.explanation: There are 7 cervical vertebrae (C1–C7) in the neck region.

quiz_questions.question_text: True ribs are ribs numbered:
quiz_questions.explanation: True ribs (1–7) attach directly to the sternum through their own costal cartilage.

quiz_questions.question_text: The hyoid bone is unique because it:

options.text: Does not articulate with any other bone

quiz_questions.explanation: The hyoid is the only bone in the body that does not directly articulate with another bone.

quiz_questions.question_text: Intervertebral discs are made primarily of:
quiz_questions.explanation: Intervertebral discs are fibrocartilaginous structures that absorb shock between vertebrae.

quiz_questions.question_text: The cranium protects the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The 8 cranial bones form a protective vault around the brain.

quiz_questions.question_text: Floating ribs are ribs number:
quiz_questions.explanation: Floating ribs (11 and 12) lack any anterior attachment to the sternum.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: The Appendicular Skeleton
summary_sections.content_html:
The appendicular skeleton (126 bones) includes the limbs and their girdles:

  • Pectoral (shoulder) girdle: clavicle and scapula; attaches upper limbs to the axial skeleton
  • Upper limbs: humerus (arm), radius and ulna (forearm), carpals (8 wrist bones), metacarpals (5 palm bones), phalanges (14 finger bones)
  • Pelvic girdle: two hip bones (ilium, ischium, pubis fused); supports body weight and protects pelvic organs
  • Lower limbs: femur (thigh — longest/strongest bone), patella (kneecap), tibia and fibula (leg), tarsals (7 ankle), metatarsals (5 foot bones), phalanges (14 toes)

flashcards.question: How many bones in the appendicular skeleton?
flashcards.answer: 126 bones

flashcards.question: What is the longest and strongest bone in the body?
flashcards.answer: The femur (thigh bone)

flashcards.question: What bones make up the pectoral girdle?
flashcards.answer: The clavicle (collarbone) and scapula (shoulder blade)

flashcards.question: How many carpal bones are in each wrist?
flashcards.answer: 8 carpal bones

flashcards.question: What three bones fuse to form each hip bone?
flashcards.answer: Ilium, ischium, and pubis

quiz_questions.question_text: The appendicular skeleton consists of:
quiz_questions.explanation: The appendicular skeleton has 126 bones — the limbs and their girdles.

quiz_questions.question_text: The longest bone in the body is the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The femur (thigh bone) is both the longest and strongest bone in the human body.

quiz_questions.question_text: The radius and ulna are found in the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The radius (lateral) and ulna (medial) are the two bones of the forearm.

quiz_questions.question_text: The pelvic girdle is formed by the fusion of:
quiz_questions.explanation: Each hip bone is formed by the fusion of the ilium, ischium, and pubis.

quiz_questions.question_text: The patella is commonly known as the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The patella is a sesamoid bone that protects the front of the knee joint.

quiz_questions.question_text: Each hand has how many phalanges?
quiz_questions.explanation: Each hand has 14 phalanges: 2 in the thumb and 3 in each of the other four fingers.

quiz_questions.question_text: The clavicle is also called the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The clavicle (collarbone) connects the scapula to the sternum.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Joints & Movement
summary_sections.content_html:
Joints (articulations) are where two or more bones meet:

  • Fibrous joints: immovable (synarthroses); bones joined by dense fibrous tissue (e.g., skull sutures)
  • Cartilaginous joints: slightly movable (amphiarthroses); bones connected by cartilage (e.g., intervertebral discs, pubic symphysis)
  • Synovial joints: freely movable (diarthroses); enclosed in a joint capsule with synovial fluid for lubrication
  • Types of synovial joints: ball-and-socket (hip, shoulder), hinge (elbow, knee), pivot (atlas/axis), saddle (thumb), gliding (carpals), condyloid (wrist)
  • Movements: flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, rotation, circumduction

flashcards.question: What is a synovial joint?
flashcards.answer: A freely movable joint enclosed in a capsule containing synovial fluid for lubrication

flashcards.question: What type of joint is the elbow?
flashcards.answer: A hinge joint — allows movement in only one plane (flexion and extension)

flashcards.question: What is the function of synovial fluid?
flashcards.answer: Lubricates the joint, reduces friction, and nourishes the articular cartilage

flashcards.question: What is flexion?
flashcards.answer: A movement that decreases the angle between two bones at a joint

flashcards.question: What type of joint are skull sutures?
flashcards.answer: Fibrous (synarthrotic) joints — immovable

quiz_questions.question_text: Synovial joints are classified as:
quiz_questions.explanation: Synovial joints (diarthroses) allow the greatest range of motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: The hip joint is an example of a:
quiz_questions.explanation: The hip joint allows multi-axial movement characteristic of ball-and-socket joints.

quiz_questions.question_text: Skull sutures are examples of _____ joints.
quiz_questions.explanation: Skull sutures are fibrous joints held together by dense connective tissue.

quiz_questions.question_text: Synovial fluid functions to:

options.text: Lubricate the joint and reduce friction

quiz_questions.explanation: Synovial fluid reduces friction between articular cartilage surfaces during movement.

quiz_questions.question_text: Flexion refers to:

options.text: Increasing the angle at a joint
Decreasing the angle between two bones

quiz_questions.explanation: Flexion decreases the joint angle, like bending the elbow.

quiz_questions.question_text: The pivot joint between atlas and axis allows:
quiz_questions.explanation: The atlas (C1) rotates around the dens of the axis (C2), allowing you to shake your head 'no'.

quiz_questions.question_text: Abduction is the movement of a limb:
quiz_questions.explanation: Abduction moves a body part away from the body's midline.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Muscle Types & Contraction
summary_sections.content_html:
Muscle Types & Contraction:

  • Skeletal muscle: voluntary, striated, attached to bones via tendons; responsible for body movement
  • Cardiac muscle: involuntary, striated, found only in the heart; has intercalated discs for synchronized contraction
  • Smooth muscle: involuntary, non-striated, found in walls of hollow organs (stomach, intestines, blood vessels)
  • Sliding filament theory: muscle contraction occurs when actin (thin filaments) and myosin (thick filaments) slide past each other, shortening the sarcomere
  • Neuromuscular junction: where a motor neuron meets a muscle fiber; acetylcholine triggers contraction
  • ATP provides the energy for muscle contraction

flashcards.question: What is the sliding filament theory?
flashcards.answer: Muscle contraction occurs when actin and myosin filaments slide past each other, shortening the sarcomere

flashcards.question: What is a sarcomere?
flashcards.answer: The basic contractile unit of skeletal muscle, from one Z-line to the next

flashcards.question: What neurotransmitter triggers muscle contraction?
flashcards.answer: Acetylcholine (ACh) released at the neuromuscular junction

flashcards.question: What type of muscle is found in blood vessel walls?
flashcards.answer: Smooth muscle — involuntary and non-striated

flashcards.question: What provides the energy for muscle contraction?
flashcards.answer: ATP (adenosine triphosphate)

quiz_questions.question_text: Which muscle type is voluntary and striated?
quiz_questions.explanation: Skeletal muscle is the only type that is both voluntary and striated.

quiz_questions.question_text: The sliding filament theory explains:
quiz_questions.explanation: The sliding filament theory describes how actin and myosin interact to produce muscle contraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Acetylcholine is released at the:
quiz_questions.explanation: At the NMJ, motor neurons release acetylcholine to stimulate muscle fiber contraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Cardiac muscle is unique because it:

options.text: Has intercalated discs and contracts involuntarily

quiz_questions.explanation: Cardiac muscle cells are connected by intercalated discs and contract without conscious control.

quiz_questions.question_text: Smooth muscle is found in the:

options.text: Walls of hollow organs like the intestines

quiz_questions.explanation: Smooth muscle lines the walls of organs, blood vessels, and passageways.

quiz_questions.question_text: The energy source for muscle contraction is:
quiz_questions.explanation: ATP provides the direct energy for the myosin-actin power stroke during contraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: A sarcomere is:

options.text: The basic contractile unit of muscle between Z-lines

quiz_questions.explanation: Each sarcomere — from Z-line to Z-line — is the fundamental unit of muscle contraction.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Major Muscle Groups
summary_sections.content_html:
Major Muscle Groups work together to produce movement:

  • Head/Neck: frontalis (raises eyebrows), orbicularis oculi (closes eyes), masseter (chewing), sternocleidomastoid (rotates/flexes head)
  • Trunk: pectoralis major (adducts arm), rectus abdominis (flexes trunk/'sit-up' muscle), external obliques (rotate trunk), erector spinae (extends spine/posture)
  • Upper limb: deltoid (abducts arm), biceps brachii (flexes elbow), triceps brachii (extends elbow)
  • Lower limb: gluteus maximus (extends hip), quadriceps (extends knee), hamstrings (flexes knee), gastrocnemius (plantar flexes ankle/'calf muscle')
  • Agonist/antagonist: muscles work in opposing pairs (e.g., biceps flexes, triceps extends)

flashcards.question: What is the agonist-antagonist relationship?
flashcards.answer: Muscles work in opposing pairs: one contracts (agonist) while the other relaxes (antagonist)

flashcards.question: What does the biceps brachii do?
flashcards.answer: Flexes the elbow and supinates the forearm

flashcards.question: What is the largest muscle in the body?
flashcards.answer: Gluteus maximus — extends and laterally rotates the hip

flashcards.question: What does the quadriceps group do?
flashcards.answer: Extends (straightens) the knee

flashcards.question: The masseter muscle is used for:
flashcards.answer: Chewing (closing the jaw) — one of the strongest muscles by force

quiz_questions.question_text: The biceps brachii and triceps brachii are examples of:
quiz_questions.explanation: The biceps flexes the elbow while the triceps extends it — they are antagonists.

quiz_questions.question_text: The quadriceps group functions to:
quiz_questions.explanation: The quadriceps (four muscles on the front of the thigh) extend the knee.

quiz_questions.question_text: The gluteus maximus is the body's largest muscle and functions to:
quiz_questions.explanation: The gluteus maximus powerfully extends the hip, important for climbing and standing from sitting.

quiz_questions.question_text: The gastrocnemius is commonly known as the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The gastrocnemius is the prominent muscle of the calf, responsible for plantar flexion.

quiz_questions.question_text: The deltoid muscle:

options.text: Abducts the arm at the shoulder

quiz_questions.explanation: The deltoid caps the shoulder and is the primary abductor of the arm.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which muscle is the primary muscle for chewing?
quiz_questions.explanation: The masseter is one of the strongest muscles for its size and is the primary chewing muscle.

quiz_questions.question_text: The rectus abdominis is responsible for:
quiz_questions.explanation: The rectus abdominis runs vertically along the front of the abdomen and flexes the trunk.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Neuron Structure & Function
summary_sections.content_html:
Neurons are the functional units of the nervous system:

  • Cell body (soma): contains the nucleus and most organelles
  • Dendrites: short branching extensions that receive signals from other neurons
  • Axon: long fiber that transmits impulses away from the cell body
  • Myelin sheath: insulating fatty covering on many axons; speeds up signal transmission (saltatory conduction)
  • Nodes of Ranvier: gaps in the myelin sheath where the impulse 'jumps'
  • Types: sensory (afferent), motor (efferent), and interneurons (connecting neurons)

flashcards.question: What do dendrites do?
flashcards.answer: Receive incoming signals from other neurons and transmit them toward the cell body

flashcards.question: What is the myelin sheath?
flashcards.answer: A fatty insulating layer around axons that speeds up nerve impulse transmission

flashcards.question: What are Nodes of Ranvier?
flashcards.answer: Gaps in the myelin sheath where the action potential is regenerated, enabling saltatory conduction

flashcards.question: What is the difference between sensory and motor neurons?
flashcards.answer: Sensory (afferent) neurons carry signals TO the CNS; motor (efferent) neurons carry signals FROM the CNS

flashcards.question: What is the axon terminal?
flashcards.answer: The end of the axon where neurotransmitters are released into the synaptic cleft

quiz_questions.question_text: Dendrites function to:

options.text: Transmit signals away from the cell body

quiz_questions.explanation: Dendrites receive incoming signals and conduct them toward the cell body.

quiz_questions.question_text: The myelin sheath speeds up nerve impulses through:
quiz_questions.explanation: Saltatory conduction is the jumping of the impulse from one Node of Ranvier to the next.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sensory neurons are also called:
quiz_questions.explanation: Sensory neurons are afferent — they carry information toward the central nervous system.

quiz_questions.question_text: The cell body of a neuron contains the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The cell body (soma) houses the nucleus and organelles needed for cell function.

quiz_questions.question_text: An axon transmits impulses:
quiz_questions.explanation: Axons carry nerve impulses away from the soma toward the axon terminals.

quiz_questions.question_text: Motor neurons carry signals:

options.text: From the CNS to muscles or glands

quiz_questions.explanation: Motor (efferent) neurons transmit commands from the CNS to effectors (muscles, glands).

quiz_questions.question_text: Interneurons are found primarily in the:

options.text: Central nervous system (brain and spinal cord)

quiz_questions.explanation: Interneurons connect sensory and motor neurons within the CNS.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Nerve Impulse Transmission
summary_sections.content_html:
Nerve impulse transmission involves electrical and chemical signals:

  • Resting potential: ~-70mV; the inside of the neuron is negative relative to outside (due to Na⁺/K⁺ pump)
  • Action potential: when stimulated past threshold, Na⁺ channels open (depolarization to ~+30mV), then K⁺ channels open (repolarization)
  • All-or-none principle: a neuron either fires completely or not at all
  • Synapse: the junction between two neurons; neurotransmitters cross the synaptic cleft
  • Neurotransmitters: chemical messengers (e.g., acetylcholine, dopamine, serotonin, GABA, norepinephrine)

flashcards.question: What is resting potential?
flashcards.answer: The electrical charge difference (~-70mV) across a neuron's membrane when it is not transmitting a signal

flashcards.question: What happens during depolarization?
flashcards.answer: Sodium (Na⁺) channels open, Na⁺ rushes in, and the inside becomes more positive

flashcards.question: What is the all-or-none principle?
flashcards.answer: A neuron either fires a full action potential or does not fire at all — there is no partial response

flashcards.question: What is a synapse?
flashcards.answer: The gap (junction) between two neurons where neurotransmitters transmit the signal

flashcards.question: Name three common neurotransmitters.
flashcards.answer: Acetylcholine, dopamine, serotonin

quiz_questions.question_text: The resting membrane potential of a neuron is approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: At rest, the inside of the neuron is about -70mV relative to the outside.

quiz_questions.question_text: During depolarization, which ion rushes into the neuron?
quiz_questions.explanation: Depolarization occurs when voltage-gated sodium channels open and Na⁺ flows inward.

quiz_questions.question_text: The all-or-none principle states that:

options.text: Neurons can fire at varying strengths
A neuron either fires fully or not at all
Only sensory neurons follow this rule

quiz_questions.explanation: If the stimulus reaches threshold, the neuron fires a complete action potential; below threshold, nothing happens.

quiz_questions.question_text: Neurotransmitters cross the _____ to reach the next neuron.
quiz_questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters are released into the synaptic cleft — the gap between neurons — and bind to receptors on the postsynaptic cell.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Na⁺/K⁺ pump helps maintain resting potential by:

options.text: Pumping 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in

quiz_questions.explanation: The Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pumps 3 sodium ions out for every 2 potassium ions in, maintaining the negative resting potential.

quiz_questions.question_text: Dopamine is an example of a:
quiz_questions.explanation: Dopamine is a neurotransmitter involved in reward, motivation, and motor control.

quiz_questions.question_text: Repolarization involves:

options.text: K⁺ flowing out of the neuron

quiz_questions.explanation: During repolarization, potassium channels open and K⁺ exits the cell, restoring the negative charge.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Central Nervous System (Brain & Spinal Cord)
summary_sections.content_html:
The Central Nervous System (CNS) consists of the brain and spinal cord:

  • Brain: ~86 billion neurons; protected by skull, meninges, and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)
    • Cerebrum: largest part; handles thinking, memory, sensory processing, voluntary movement. Divided into left/right hemispheres, each with 4 lobes (frontal, parietal, temporal, occipital)
    • Cerebellum: coordinates balance, posture, and smooth movement
    • Brainstem: (midbrain, pons, medulla oblongata) controls vital functions: breathing, heart rate, consciousness
  • Spinal cord: extends from brainstem to L1-L2; relays signals between brain and body; mediates reflexes

flashcards.question: What are the four lobes of the cerebrum?
flashcards.answer: Frontal (reasoning/motor), parietal (sensation), temporal (hearing/memory), occipital (vision)

flashcards.question: What does the cerebellum do?
flashcards.answer: Coordinates balance, posture, and smooth voluntary movements

flashcards.question: What does the medulla oblongata control?
flashcards.answer: Vital involuntary functions: heart rate, breathing, blood pressure

flashcards.question: What is cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)?
flashcards.answer: A clear fluid that cushions the brain and spinal cord, provides nutrients, and removes waste

flashcards.question: What are meninges?
flashcards.answer: Three protective membranes (dura mater, arachnoid, pia mater) covering the brain and spinal cord

quiz_questions.question_text: The largest part of the brain is the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The cerebrum makes up about 85% of the brain's weight and handles higher functions.

quiz_questions.question_text: The occipital lobe is primarily responsible for:
quiz_questions.explanation: The occipital lobe, at the back of the brain, processes visual information.

quiz_questions.question_text: The cerebellum coordinates:
quiz_questions.explanation: The cerebellum fine-tunes motor activity and maintains balance and posture.

quiz_questions.question_text: The medulla oblongata controls:

options.text: Breathing, heart rate, and blood pressure

quiz_questions.explanation: The medulla controls vital autonomic functions essential for survival.

quiz_questions.question_text: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) functions to:

options.text: Cushion and protect the brain and spinal cord

quiz_questions.explanation: CSF acts as a shock absorber and provides nutrients to neural tissue.

quiz_questions.question_text: The spinal cord extends from the brainstem to approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: The spinal cord ends at approximately the first or second lumbar vertebra.

quiz_questions.question_text: The frontal lobe is involved in:
quiz_questions.explanation: The frontal lobe handles executive functions, decision-making, and controls voluntary motor activity.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Peripheral Nervous System
summary_sections.content_html:
The Peripheral Nervous System (PNS) connects the CNS to the rest of the body:

  • Somatic nervous system: voluntary control of skeletal muscles; 12 pairs of cranial nerves, 31 pairs of spinal nerves
  • Autonomic nervous system (ANS): involuntary control of internal organs
    • Sympathetic division: 'fight or flight' — increases heart rate, dilates pupils, inhibits digestion
    • Parasympathetic division: 'rest and digest' — slows heart rate, constricts pupils, promotes digestion
  • Reflex arc: rapid, involuntary response; receptor → afferent neuron → integration center → efferent neuron → effector

flashcards.question: What is the sympathetic nervous system?
flashcards.answer: The 'fight or flight' division that prepares the body for stressful or emergency situations

flashcards.question: What is the parasympathetic nervous system?
flashcards.answer: The 'rest and digest' division that conserves energy and promotes routine body maintenance

flashcards.question: How many pairs of spinal nerves exist?
flashcards.answer: 31 pairs of spinal nerves

flashcards.question: What is a reflex arc?
flashcards.answer: A rapid, involuntary neural pathway: receptor → sensory neuron → integration center → motor neuron → effector

flashcards.question: What is the somatic nervous system?
flashcards.answer: The division of the PNS that controls voluntary skeletal muscle movements

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'fight or flight' response is controlled by the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The sympathetic division activates the body's stress response.

quiz_questions.question_text: The parasympathetic division promotes:
quiz_questions.explanation: The parasympathetic system slows the heart, promotes digestion — 'rest and digest.'

quiz_questions.question_text: How many pairs of cranial nerves are there?
quiz_questions.explanation: There are 12 pairs of cranial nerves that emerge directly from the brain.

quiz_questions.question_text: A reflex arc is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Reflexes are fast, automatic responses that don't require brain processing.

quiz_questions.question_text: The somatic nervous system controls:
quiz_questions.explanation: The somatic NS provides voluntary control over skeletal muscle movements.

quiz_questions.question_text: During sympathetic activation, the pupils:
quiz_questions.explanation: Sympathetic activation dilates the pupils to let in more light during a stress response.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a reflex arc, the integration center is typically in the:
quiz_questions.explanation: Most reflex arcs are processed in the spinal cord, enabling rapid response without brain involvement.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: The Senses (Vision, Hearing, Touch)
summary_sections.content_html:
The Senses are the body's windows to the external world:

  • Vision: light enters the eye through the cornea → pupil → lens focuses on retina → rods (dim light/peripheral) and cones (color/detail) → optic nerve to brain
  • Hearing: sound waves → outer ear → tympanic membrane (eardrum) → ossicles (malleus, incus, stapes) → cochlea (fluid-filled, contains hair cells) → auditory nerve
  • Touch: mechanoreceptors in the skin detect pressure, vibration, and texture. Thermoreceptors detect temperature. Nociceptors detect pain.
  • Taste: taste buds on the tongue detect sweet, salty, sour, bitter, umami
  • Smell (olfaction): olfactory receptors in the nasal cavity detect airborne chemicals

flashcards.question: What are rods and cones?
flashcards.answer: Photoreceptor cells in the retina: rods detect dim light, cones detect color

flashcards.question: What are the three middle ear bones?
flashcards.answer: Malleus (hammer), incus (anvil), stapes (stirrup) — the ossicles

flashcards.question: What is the cochlea?
flashcards.answer: A snail-shaped, fluid-filled structure in the inner ear that converts sound vibrations into nerve signals

flashcards.question: What are nociceptors?
flashcards.answer: Pain receptors that detect tissue damage or potentially harmful stimuli

flashcards.question: What are the five basic tastes?
flashcards.answer: Sweet, salty, sour, bitter, and umami (savory)

quiz_questions.question_text: Rods in the retina are responsible for:
quiz_questions.explanation: Rods are highly sensitive to light and enable vision in low-light conditions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sound is converted to nerve impulses in the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The cochlea's hair cells convert mechanical vibrations into electrical nerve signals.

quiz_questions.question_text: The smallest bone in the human body is the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The stapes (stirrup) in the middle ear is the smallest bone in the body.

quiz_questions.question_text: Nociceptors detect:
quiz_questions.explanation: Nociceptors are specialized sensory receptors for potentially damaging stimuli (pain).

quiz_questions.question_text: The five basic taste sensations include all EXCEPT:
quiz_questions.explanation: Spicy is not a taste — it's a pain/heat sensation detected by nociceptors, not taste buds.

quiz_questions.question_text: Light is focused onto the retina by the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The lens adjusts its shape (accommodation) to focus light precisely on the retina.

quiz_questions.question_text: Olfactory receptors are located in the:
quiz_questions.explanation: Olfactory receptors in the upper nasal cavity detect airborne chemical molecules.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Disorders of the Nervous System
summary_sections.content_html:
Disorders of the Nervous System:

  • Alzheimer's disease: progressive neurodegeneration causing memory loss, confusion, personality changes; associated with amyloid plaques and tau tangles
  • Parkinson's disease: loss of dopamine-producing neurons in the substantia nigra; causes tremors, rigidity, slow movement
  • Multiple sclerosis (MS): autoimmune destruction of myelin sheaths; disrupts nerve signal transmission
  • Epilepsy: abnormal electrical activity in the brain causing seizures
  • Stroke (CVA): blood flow to the brain is blocked (ischemic) or a vessel bursts (hemorrhagic); brain cells die from oxygen deprivation
  • Concussion: mild traumatic brain injury from impact; symptoms include headache, confusion, memory issues

flashcards.question: What causes Parkinson's disease?
flashcards.answer: Loss of dopamine-producing neurons in the substantia nigra, leading to tremors and movement difficulties

flashcards.question: What is multiple sclerosis?
flashcards.answer: An autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks and destroys the myelin sheath around nerve fibers

flashcards.question: What are the two types of stroke?
flashcards.answer: Ischemic (blocked blood vessel) and hemorrhagic (burst blood vessel)

flashcards.question: What is epilepsy?
flashcards.answer: A neurological disorder characterized by recurrent seizures due to abnormal electrical activity in the brain

flashcards.question: What protein accumulations are associated with Alzheimer's?
flashcards.answer: Amyloid-beta plaques and neurofibrillary (tau) tangles

quiz_questions.question_text: Parkinson's disease is caused by the loss of:
quiz_questions.explanation: Parkinson's results from degeneration of dopamine neurons in the substantia nigra.

quiz_questions.question_text: Multiple sclerosis involves the destruction of:
quiz_questions.explanation: In MS, the immune system attacks myelin, disrupting nerve signal transmission.

quiz_questions.question_text: An ischemic stroke is caused by:

options.text: A blocked blood vessel in the brain

quiz_questions.explanation: Ischemic strokes (~87% of all strokes) occur when a clot blocks blood flow to the brain.

quiz_questions.question_text: Alzheimer's disease primarily affects:
quiz_questions.explanation: Alzheimer's is characterized by progressive memory loss and cognitive decline.

quiz_questions.question_text: Epileptic seizures are caused by:

options.text: Abnormal electrical activity in the brain

quiz_questions.explanation: Seizures result from sudden, excessive electrical discharges in the brain.

quiz_questions.question_text: A concussion is classified as:
quiz_questions.explanation: A concussion is a mild TBI caused by a blow or jolt to the head.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which disorder is autoimmune in nature?
quiz_questions.explanation: MS is an autoimmune condition where the body's immune system attacks its own myelin.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Heart Anatomy & the Cardiac Cycle
summary_sections.content_html:
Heart Anatomy & the Cardiac Cycle:

  • The heart has 4 chambers: right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, left ventricle
  • Right side pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs (pulmonary circuit)
  • Left side pumps oxygenated blood to the body (systemic circuit)
  • Valves: tricuspid (right AV), bicuspid/mitral (left AV), pulmonary semilunar, aortic semilunar — prevent backflow
  • Cardiac cycle: systole (contraction) and diastole (relaxation); SA node (pacemaker) initiates each heartbeat
  • Average resting heart rate: 60–100 bpm; cardiac output = heart rate × stroke volume

flashcards.question: What are the four chambers of the heart?
flashcards.answer: Right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, left ventricle

flashcards.question: What is the SA node?
flashcards.answer: The sinoatrial node — the heart's natural pacemaker that initiates the electrical impulse for each heartbeat

flashcards.question: What is systole?
flashcards.answer: The contraction phase of the cardiac cycle when the heart pumps blood

flashcards.question: What is cardiac output?
flashcards.answer: The volume of blood pumped by the heart per minute: heart rate × stroke volume

flashcards.question: What do heart valves do?
flashcards.answer: Prevent the backflow of blood, ensuring one-way flow through the heart

quiz_questions.question_text: The heart's natural pacemaker is the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The sinoatrial (SA) node in the right atrium initiates the electrical impulse that triggers heartbeat.

quiz_questions.question_text: Deoxygenated blood enters the heart through the:
quiz_questions.explanation: Deoxygenated blood from the body returns to the right atrium via the superior and inferior vena cava.

quiz_questions.question_text: The valve between the left atrium and left ventricle is the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The mitral (bicuspid) valve separates the left atrium from the left ventricle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Systole refers to:
quiz_questions.explanation: Systole is the contraction phase when ventricles pump blood out of the heart.

quiz_questions.question_text: The left ventricle pumps blood into the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The left ventricle pumps oxygenated blood through the aortic valve into the aorta for systemic circulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Cardiac output equals:
quiz_questions.explanation: Cardiac output (mL/min) is the product of heart rate (beats/min) and stroke volume (mL/beat).

quiz_questions.question_text: The pulmonary circuit carries blood from the heart to the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The pulmonary circuit transports deoxygenated blood to the lungs for gas exchange.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Blood Vessels & Circulation
summary_sections.content_html:
Blood Vessels & Circulation:

  • Arteries: carry blood AWAY from the heart; thick muscular walls; high pressure
  • Veins: carry blood TOWARD the heart; thinner walls; valves prevent backflow; lower pressure
  • Capillaries: microscopic vessels where gas/nutrient exchange occurs; walls one cell thick
  • Systemic circulation: left ventricle → aorta → body tissues → vena cava → right atrium
  • Pulmonary circulation: right ventricle → pulmonary artery → lungs → pulmonary veins → left atrium
  • Blood pressure: measured as systolic/diastolic (e.g., 120/80 mmHg); normal < 120/80

flashcards.question: What is the difference between arteries and veins?
flashcards.answer: Arteries carry blood away from the heart (high pressure, thick walls); veins carry blood toward the heart (low pressure, valves)

flashcards.question: Why are capillaries important?
flashcards.answer: Their walls are one cell thick, allowing gas and nutrient exchange between blood and tissues

flashcards.question: What is normal blood pressure?
flashcards.answer: Less than 120/80 mmHg (systolic/diastolic)

flashcards.question: What is systemic circulation?
flashcards.answer: The loop that carries oxygenated blood from the left heart to the body and returns deoxygenated blood to the right heart

flashcards.question: Why do veins have valves?
flashcards.answer: To prevent backflow of blood due to the low-pressure return flow to the heart

quiz_questions.question_text: Arteries carry blood:
quiz_questions.explanation: By definition, arteries carry blood away from the heart (whether oxygenated or not).

quiz_questions.question_text: Gas exchange between blood and tissues occurs in:
quiz_questions.explanation: Capillary walls are one cell thick, enabling diffusion of O₂, CO₂, and nutrients.

quiz_questions.question_text: Normal blood pressure is approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: A blood pressure reading below 120/80 mmHg is considered normal for adults.

quiz_questions.question_text: Veins have valves to:
quiz_questions.explanation: Venous valves ensure blood flows in one direction — toward the heart — despite low pressure.

quiz_questions.question_text: In pulmonary circulation, oxygenated blood returns to the heart via the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood from the lungs back to the left atrium.

quiz_questions.question_text: The largest artery in the body is the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The aorta arises from the left ventricle and is the largest artery.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which vessels have the thickest walls?
quiz_questions.explanation: Arteries have thick muscular and elastic walls to withstand high-pressure blood flow.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Blood Components & Typing
summary_sections.content_html:
Blood Components & Typing:

  • Plasma (~55% of blood): liquid portion; water, proteins (albumin, fibrinogen, antibodies), electrolytes, nutrients, waste
  • Red blood cells (erythrocytes): contain hemoglobin to carry O₂; biconcave disc shape; no nucleus; ~120-day lifespan
  • White blood cells (leukocytes): fight infection; types include neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils
  • Platelets (thrombocytes): cell fragments involved in blood clotting
  • Blood types: ABO system (A, B, AB, O) based on surface antigens; Rh factor (+/-); type O- is universal donor; AB+ is universal recipient

flashcards.question: What is hemoglobin?
flashcards.answer: An iron-containing protein in red blood cells that binds and transports oxygen

flashcards.question: What are platelets?
flashcards.answer: Cell fragments (thrombocytes) essential for blood clotting

flashcards.question: Who is the universal donor?
flashcards.answer: Type O negative (O-) — no A, B, or Rh antigens on the red blood cells

flashcards.question: What makes up plasma?
flashcards.answer: ~92% water plus proteins (albumin, fibrinogen, antibodies), electrolytes, nutrients, and waste products

flashcards.question: What is the Rh factor?
flashcards.answer: A protein on the surface of red blood cells; Rh+ means present, Rh- means absent

quiz_questions.question_text: Blood plasma makes up approximately what percentage of blood?
quiz_questions.explanation: Plasma constitutes about 55% of blood volume; formed elements make up the rest.

quiz_questions.question_text: Hemoglobin is found in:
quiz_questions.explanation: Hemoglobin is the oxygen-carrying pigment located within erythrocytes.

quiz_questions.question_text: The universal blood donor type is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Type O- lacks A, B, and Rh antigens, making it safe for any recipient.

quiz_questions.question_text: Platelets are primarily responsible for:
quiz_questions.explanation: Platelets aggregate at wound sites and initiate the clotting cascade.

quiz_questions.question_text: Red blood cells have a lifespan of approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: Erythrocytes circulate for about 120 days before being removed by the spleen.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which white blood cell type is most abundant?
quiz_questions.explanation: Neutrophils are the most common WBCs, forming the first line of defense against bacterial infection.

quiz_questions.question_text: The universal recipient blood type is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Type AB+ can receive blood from all types because it has A, B, and Rh antigens (no antibodies against them).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Respiratory Anatomy
summary_sections.content_html:
Respiratory Anatomy:

  • Upper respiratory tract: nasal cavity (warms, moistens, filters air), pharynx (throat), larynx (voice box, contains vocal cords)
  • Lower respiratory tract: trachea (windpipe, reinforced with C-shaped cartilage rings), bronchi (two main branches into each lung), bronchioles (smaller airways), alveoli
  • Alveoli: tiny air sacs (~300 million) where gas exchange occurs; surrounded by capillaries; walls one cell thick
  • Lungs: right lung has 3 lobes, left has 2; covered by pleural membranes with pleural fluid for lubrication
  • Diaphragm: dome-shaped muscle below the lungs; primary muscle of breathing

flashcards.question: What are alveoli?
flashcards.answer: Tiny air sacs in the lungs (~300 million) where oxygen and carbon dioxide are exchanged with the blood

flashcards.question: How many lobes does each lung have?
flashcards.answer: Right lung: 3 lobes; left lung: 2 lobes (to accommodate the heart)

flashcards.question: What is the diaphragm?
flashcards.answer: The dome-shaped skeletal muscle below the lungs that is the primary muscle of breathing

flashcards.question: What is the function of C-shaped cartilage rings in the trachea?
flashcards.answer: To keep the trachea open for airflow while allowing flexibility

flashcards.question: What is the larynx?
flashcards.answer: The voice box — contains vocal cords and connects the pharynx to the trachea

quiz_questions.question_text: Gas exchange occurs primarily in the:
quiz_questions.explanation: Alveoli are the tiny air sacs where O₂ diffuses into blood and CO₂ diffuses out.

quiz_questions.question_text: The right lung has _____ lobes.
quiz_questions.explanation: The right lung has three lobes (superior, middle, inferior); the left has two.

quiz_questions.question_text: The primary muscle of breathing is the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The diaphragm contracts and flattens during inhalation, creating negative pressure that draws air in.

quiz_questions.question_text: The trachea is kept open by:
quiz_questions.explanation: C-shaped cartilage rings prevent the trachea from collapsing while allowing the esophagus to expand behind it.

quiz_questions.question_text: The larynx contains:
quiz_questions.explanation: The larynx houses the vocal cords, which vibrate to produce sound.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many alveoli are estimated in the lungs?
quiz_questions.explanation: Approximately 300 million alveoli provide a huge surface area (~70 m²) for gas exchange.

quiz_questions.question_text: Pleural fluid between the pleural membranes serves to:
quiz_questions.explanation: Pleural fluid lubricates the pleural surfaces so the lungs can expand and contract smoothly.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Gas Exchange & Breathing Mechanics
summary_sections.content_html:
Gas Exchange & Breathing Mechanics:

  • Inhalation: diaphragm contracts (flattens), intercostal muscles contract, rib cage expands → thoracic volume increases → air pressure decreases → air flows in
  • Exhalation: diaphragm relaxes (domes up), rib cage retracts → thoracic volume decreases → air pressure increases → air flows out (passive at rest)
  • External respiration: O₂ diffuses from alveoli into blood capillaries; CO₂ diffuses from blood into alveoli
  • Internal respiration: O₂ diffuses from blood into body cells; CO₂ diffuses from cells into blood
  • Transport: ~98.5% of O₂ carried by hemoglobin; CO₂ carried as bicarbonate ions (~70%), bound to hemoglobin (~23%), or dissolved in plasma (~7%)

flashcards.question: What happens to the diaphragm during inhalation?
flashcards.answer: It contracts and flattens, increasing thoracic volume and drawing air into the lungs

flashcards.question: What is external respiration?
flashcards.answer: Gas exchange between the air in the alveoli and the blood in pulmonary capillaries

flashcards.question: How is most oxygen transported in the blood?
flashcards.answer: Bound to hemoglobin (~98.5%) in red blood cells

flashcards.question: What drives gas exchange?
flashcards.answer: Diffusion — gases move from areas of higher partial pressure to lower partial pressure

flashcards.question: Is normal exhalation active or passive?
flashcards.answer: Passive — the diaphragm and intercostals relax, and elastic recoil expels air

quiz_questions.question_text: During inhalation, the diaphragm:
quiz_questions.explanation: Diaphragm contraction increases thoracic volume, creating negative pressure that pulls air in.

quiz_questions.question_text: Gas exchange between alveoli and blood is driven by:
quiz_questions.explanation: Gases diffuse from high to low partial pressure across the thin alveolar-capillary membrane.

quiz_questions.question_text: Most oxygen in the blood is transported by:
quiz_questions.explanation: About 98.5% of O₂ binds to hemoglobin for transport.

quiz_questions.question_text: Internal respiration occurs:

options.text: At the body's cells and tissues

quiz_questions.explanation: Internal respiration is the exchange of gases between blood capillaries and body tissues.

quiz_questions.question_text: The majority of CO₂ is transported in the blood as:
quiz_questions.explanation: About 70% of CO₂ is converted to bicarbonate ions by carbonic anhydrase in red blood cells.

quiz_questions.question_text: Normal exhalation at rest is:
quiz_questions.explanation: At rest, exhalation is passive — the diaphragm and intercostals relax, and lung elastic recoil pushes air out.

quiz_questions.question_text: When thoracic volume increases during inhalation, air pressure inside the chest:
quiz_questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: as volume increases, pressure decreases — causing air to flow into the lungs.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Common Cardiovascular Diseases
summary_sections.content_html:
Common Cardiovascular Diseases:

  • Atherosclerosis: buildup of plaque (cholesterol, fat, calcium) inside artery walls; narrows arteries and restricts blood flow
  • Coronary artery disease (CAD): atherosclerosis in coronary arteries; leading cause of heart attack
  • Heart attack (myocardial infarction): blockage of coronary artery → heart muscle cells die from oxygen deprivation
  • Hypertension: chronically elevated blood pressure (>130/80 mmHg); 'silent killer' often with no symptoms
  • Heart failure: heart cannot pump enough blood to meet body's needs; not a complete stoppage
  • Risk factors: smoking, high cholesterol, obesity, diabetes, sedentary lifestyle, family history, stress

flashcards.question: What is atherosclerosis?
flashcards.answer: The buildup of plaque (fat, cholesterol, calcium) inside artery walls, narrowing and hardening the arteries

flashcards.question: What causes a heart attack?
flashcards.answer: Blockage of a coronary artery (usually by a blood clot on plaque), cutting off oxygen to heart muscle

flashcards.question: What is hypertension?
flashcards.answer: Chronically elevated blood pressure above 130/80 mmHg — often called the 'silent killer'

flashcards.question: What is heart failure?
flashcards.answer: A condition where the heart cannot pump enough blood to meet the body's needs (it does not stop beating)

flashcards.question: Name three risk factors for cardiovascular disease.
flashcards.answer: Smoking, high cholesterol, and obesity (plus diabetes, sedentary lifestyle, family history)

quiz_questions.question_text: Atherosclerosis is the buildup of _____ in artery walls.
quiz_questions.explanation: Atherosclerotic plaque consists of lipids, cholesterol, calcium, and cellular debris.

quiz_questions.question_text: A myocardial infarction (heart attack) occurs when:

options.text: A coronary artery is blocked, starving heart tissue of oxygen

quiz_questions.explanation: A heart attack results from coronary artery blockage, causing ischemia and death of cardiac muscle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Hypertension is defined as blood pressure above:
quiz_questions.explanation: Current guidelines define hypertension as systolic ≥130 or diastolic ≥80 mmHg.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is a modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease?
quiz_questions.explanation: Smoking is modifiable — quitting significantly reduces cardiovascular risk.

quiz_questions.question_text: Heart failure means:

options.text: The heart cannot pump enough blood to meet the body's needs

quiz_questions.explanation: Heart failure is a condition of inadequate cardiac output, not cardiac arrest.

quiz_questions.question_text: The leading cause of heart attacks is:
quiz_questions.explanation: CAD — plaque buildup in coronary arteries — is the primary cause of heart attacks.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why is hypertension called the 'silent killer'?

options.text: It often has no noticeable symptoms until serious damage occurs

quiz_questions.explanation: Many people with hypertension have no symptoms, yet it damages blood vessels and organs over time.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Organs of the Digestive Tract
summary_sections.content_html:
Organs of the Digestive Tract (GI tract):

  • Mouth: mechanical digestion (teeth) and chemical digestion (salivary amylase breaks down starch)
  • Esophagus: muscular tube; moves food via peristalsis (wave-like contractions)
  • Stomach: churns food with gastric juice (HCl + pepsin); produces chyme
  • Small intestine: ~20 feet; primary site of digestion and nutrient absorption; three sections: duodenum, jejunum, ileum; lined with villi and microvilli
  • Large intestine (colon): absorbs water and electrolytes; houses gut bacteria; forms feces
  • Rectum & anus: store and eliminate feces

flashcards.question: What is peristalsis?
flashcards.answer: Wave-like muscle contractions that move food through the digestive tract

flashcards.question: Where does most digestion and absorption occur?
flashcards.answer: In the small intestine, especially the duodenum and jejunum

flashcards.question: What are villi?
flashcards.answer: Finger-like projections lining the small intestine that increase surface area for absorption

flashcards.question: What does the stomach produce?
flashcards.answer: Gastric juice containing hydrochloric acid (HCl) and pepsin to digest protein

flashcards.question: What is the main function of the large intestine?
flashcards.answer: Absorbing water and electrolytes from remaining food matter and forming feces

quiz_questions.question_text: The primary site of nutrient absorption is the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The small intestine's enormous surface area (villi and microvilli) makes it the main absorption site.

quiz_questions.question_text: Peristalsis is:

options.text: Wave-like muscle contractions that move food

quiz_questions.explanation: Peristalsis propels food through the GI tract via rhythmic contractions of smooth muscle.

quiz_questions.question_text: The stomach produces hydrochloric acid (HCl) to:

options.text: Kill bacteria and activate pepsin for protein digestion

quiz_questions.explanation: HCl creates an acidic environment (pH ~2) that kills pathogens and activates pepsinogen to pepsin.

quiz_questions.question_text: Villi and microvilli in the small intestine function to:

options.text: Greatly increase surface area for absorption

quiz_questions.explanation: These structures increase the absorptive surface area to approximately 200 m².

quiz_questions.question_text: The three sections of the small intestine in order are:
quiz_questions.explanation: Food passes from duodenum to jejunum to ileum.

quiz_questions.question_text: The large intestine primarily absorbs:
quiz_questions.explanation: The colon's main function is reabsorbing water to form solid feces.

quiz_questions.question_text: Chemical digestion in the mouth begins with:
quiz_questions.explanation: Salivary amylase begins the chemical breakdown of starch into maltose in the mouth.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Chemical & Mechanical Digestion
summary_sections.content_html:
Chemical & Mechanical Digestion:

  • Mechanical digestion: physical breakdown without changing chemical structure — chewing (mastication), churning in stomach, segmentation in small intestine
  • Chemical digestion: enzymes break chemical bonds in food molecules
    • Carbohydrates: amylase (mouth, pancreas) → maltase → glucose
    • Proteins: pepsin (stomach), trypsin/chymotrypsin (pancreas) → amino acids
    • Fats: bile (emulsifies) + lipase (pancreas) → fatty acids and glycerol
  • Bile: produced by the liver, stored in gallbladder; emulsifies fats (breaks them into smaller droplets)

flashcards.question: What is the difference between mechanical and chemical digestion?
flashcards.answer: Mechanical physically breaks food into smaller pieces; chemical uses enzymes to break molecular bonds

flashcards.question: What enzyme digests starch?
flashcards.answer: Amylase — found in saliva and pancreatic juice

flashcards.question: What does bile do?
flashcards.answer: Emulsifies fats — breaks large fat globules into smaller droplets for enzyme access

flashcards.question: Where is bile produced and stored?
flashcards.answer: Produced by the liver, stored in the gallbladder

flashcards.question: What are the end products of protein digestion?
flashcards.answer: Amino acids

quiz_questions.question_text: Mechanical digestion includes all EXCEPT:
quiz_questions.explanation: Enzyme action is chemical digestion; chewing, churning, and segmentation are mechanical.

quiz_questions.question_text: Amylase breaks down:
quiz_questions.explanation: Amylase is a carbohydrate-digesting enzyme that breaks starch into maltose.

quiz_questions.question_text: Bile functions to:
quiz_questions.explanation: Bile emulsifies fats, increasing surface area for lipase to work.

quiz_questions.question_text: Proteins are ultimately broken down into:
quiz_questions.explanation: Protein digestion yields individual amino acids for absorption.

quiz_questions.question_text: Pepsin is most active in:
quiz_questions.explanation: Pepsin works optimally at the low pH (~2) of the stomach.

quiz_questions.question_text: Lipase breaks down fats into:
quiz_questions.explanation: Pancreatic lipase hydrolyzes triglycerides into fatty acids and glycerol.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which organ produces bile?
quiz_questions.explanation: The liver produces bile; the gallbladder stores and concentrates it.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Nutrient Absorption
summary_sections.content_html:
Nutrient Absorption occurs primarily in the small intestine:

  • Carbohydrates: absorbed as monosaccharides (glucose, fructose, galactose) via active transport and facilitated diffusion into blood capillaries
  • Proteins: absorbed as amino acids and small peptides via active transport into blood capillaries
  • Fats: absorbed as fatty acids and monoglycerides; reassembled into triglycerides → packaged into chylomicrons → enter lacteals (lymphatic vessels)
  • Vitamins: fat-soluble (A, D, E, K) absorbed with fats; water-soluble (B, C) absorbed directly
  • Water: absorbed by osmosis in both small and large intestines (~9 L/day reabsorbed)

flashcards.question: How are fats absorbed differently from carbohydrates and proteins?
flashcards.answer: Fats enter lacteals (lymphatic vessels) as chylomicrons instead of directly entering blood capillaries

flashcards.question: What is a chylomicron?
flashcards.answer: A lipoprotein particle that transports dietary triglycerides from the intestine into the lymphatic system

flashcards.question: What are fat-soluble vitamins?
flashcards.answer: Vitamins A, D, E, and K — they require dietary fat for absorption

flashcards.question: How is glucose absorbed in the small intestine?
flashcards.answer: Primarily by active transport (sodium-glucose co-transport) across the intestinal epithelium

flashcards.question: What are lacteals?
flashcards.answer: Specialized lymphatic capillaries in the villi that absorb dietary fats

quiz_questions.question_text: Most nutrient absorption occurs in the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The small intestine is the primary site for absorbing digested nutrients.

quiz_questions.question_text: Fats are absorbed into _____ rather than blood capillaries.
quiz_questions.explanation: Dietary fats are packaged into chylomicrons that enter the lymphatic lacteals.

quiz_questions.question_text: Fat-soluble vitamins include:
quiz_questions.explanation: Vitamins A, D, E, K dissolve in fat and require lipids for absorption.

quiz_questions.question_text: Glucose is absorbed by the small intestine primarily through:
quiz_questions.explanation: Glucose absorption uses sodium-dependent active transport (SGLT1) in the intestinal epithelium.

quiz_questions.question_text: Water absorption in the digestive tract occurs by:
quiz_questions.explanation: Water follows solutes by osmosis across the intestinal wall.

quiz_questions.question_text: Amino acids enter the blood via:
quiz_questions.explanation: Amino acids are transported directly into blood capillaries of the villi through active transport.

quiz_questions.question_text: Approximately how much water is reabsorbed daily by the intestines?
quiz_questions.explanation: The GI tract reabsorbs approximately 9 liters of water per day from food and digestive secretions.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: The Liver, Pancreas & Gallbladder
summary_sections.content_html:
The Liver, Pancreas & Gallbladder are accessory digestive organs:

  • Liver: largest internal organ; produces bile; detoxifies blood; stores glycogen; metabolizes drugs and alcohol; produces blood-clotting proteins; converts ammonia to urea
  • Gallbladder: small sac beneath the liver; stores and concentrates bile; releases bile into the duodenum when fats are present
  • Pancreas: dual function organ
    • Exocrine: secretes pancreatic juice containing digestive enzymes (amylase, lipase, trypsin) and bicarbonate (neutralizes stomach acid)
    • Endocrine: Islets of Langerhans secrete insulin (lowers blood glucose) and glucagon (raises blood glucose)

flashcards.question: What are the main functions of the liver?
flashcards.answer: Produces bile, detoxifies blood, stores glycogen, metabolizes drugs, produces clotting proteins, converts ammonia to urea

flashcards.question: What does the gallbladder do?
flashcards.answer: Stores and concentrates bile produced by the liver, releasing it when fats enter the duodenum

flashcards.question: What is the exocrine function of the pancreas?
flashcards.answer: Producing digestive enzymes (amylase, lipase, trypsin) and bicarbonate to neutralize stomach acid

flashcards.question: What do the Islets of Langerhans secrete?
flashcards.answer: Insulin (lowers blood sugar) and glucagon (raises blood sugar)

flashcards.question: Why does the pancreas secrete bicarbonate?
flashcards.answer: To neutralize the acidic chyme entering the duodenum from the stomach, protecting the intestinal lining

quiz_questions.question_text: The liver produces:
quiz_questions.explanation: The liver synthesizes bile, which is essential for fat emulsification.

quiz_questions.question_text: The gallbladder functions to:
quiz_questions.explanation: The gallbladder stores bile produced by the liver and releases it when needed.

quiz_questions.question_text: Insulin is secreted by the pancreas to:
quiz_questions.explanation: Insulin allows cells to take up glucose from the blood, lowering blood sugar levels.

quiz_questions.question_text: The pancreatic enzyme lipase digests:
quiz_questions.explanation: Lipase specifically breaks down triglycerides into fatty acids and glycerol.

quiz_questions.question_text: Bicarbonate from the pancreas:

options.text: Neutralizes acidic chyme from the stomach

quiz_questions.explanation: Bicarbonate raises the pH in the duodenum, protecting the intestinal lining from stomach acid.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Islets of Langerhans are located in the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The Islets of Langerhans are clusters of endocrine cells within the pancreas.

quiz_questions.question_text: The liver converts toxic ammonia into:
quiz_questions.explanation: The liver detoxifies ammonia (from protein metabolism) by converting it to urea for excretion by the kidneys.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Urinary System & Kidney Function
summary_sections.content_html:
Urinary System & Kidney Function:

  • Kidneys (2): filter blood, remove waste, regulate water/electrolyte balance, maintain blood pH, produce erythropoietin (stimulates RBC production)
  • Nephron: functional unit of the kidney (~1 million per kidney). Components: glomerulus, Bowman's capsule, proximal tubule, loop of Henle, distal tubule, collecting duct
  • Filtration: blood filtered at the glomerulus; filtrate enters Bowman's capsule
  • Reabsorption: useful substances (glucose, amino acids, water, ions) returned to blood in tubules
  • Secretion: additional wastes/H⁺ ions transferred from blood into the tubule
  • Urine pathway: kidneys → ureters → bladder → urethra

flashcards.question: What is the nephron?
flashcards.answer: The functional unit of the kidney; filters blood and produces urine (~1 million per kidney)

flashcards.question: What are the three processes of urine formation?
flashcards.answer: Filtration (at glomerulus), reabsorption (in tubules), and secretion (into tubules)

flashcards.question: What is the glomerulus?
flashcards.answer: A cluster of capillaries in the nephron where blood is filtered under pressure

flashcards.question: What is the path of urine from the kidneys?
flashcards.answer: Kidneys → ureters → urinary bladder → urethra

flashcards.question: What hormone do the kidneys produce?
flashcards.answer: Erythropoietin (EPO) — stimulates red blood cell production in bone marrow

quiz_questions.question_text: The functional unit of the kidney is the:
quiz_questions.explanation: Each nephron independently filters blood and produces urine.

quiz_questions.question_text: Blood is filtered in the kidney at the:
quiz_questions.explanation: The glomerulus is a tuft of capillaries where blood pressure forces fluid and solutes into Bowman's capsule.

quiz_questions.question_text: Reabsorption in the nephron returns _____ to the blood.

options.text: Useful substances like glucose, water, and amino acids

quiz_questions.explanation: Tubular reabsorption reclaims valuable substances that were filtered out.

quiz_questions.question_text: The correct pathway of urine is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Urine flows from the kidneys through the ureters to the bladder and out through the urethra.

quiz_questions.question_text: Each kidney contains approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: Each kidney has about 1 million nephrons for blood filtration.

quiz_questions.question_text: The kidneys help regulate blood pH by:

options.text: Excreting hydrogen ions and reabsorbing bicarbonate

quiz_questions.explanation: Kidneys maintain acid-base balance by excreting H⁺ ions and retaining HCO₃⁻ as needed.

quiz_questions.question_text: Erythropoietin (EPO) produced by the kidneys stimulates:
quiz_questions.explanation: EPO signals the bone marrow to increase red blood cell production when oxygen levels are low.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Endocrine Glands & Hormones
summary_sections.content_html:
Endocrine Glands & Hormones:

  • Endocrine system: glands that secrete hormones directly into the bloodstream (ductless glands)
  • Major glands:
    • Hypothalamus: links nervous and endocrine systems; releases releasing/inhibiting hormones
    • Pituitary: 'master gland'; anterior produces GH, TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH, prolactin; posterior releases ADH, oxytocin
    • Thyroid: T3/T4 (metabolism), calcitonin (lowers blood calcium)
    • Parathyroids: PTH (raises blood calcium)
